content: [{"id":"3dc7e155-a455-4574-a2c9-bf1a0bf8c082","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Serialization is a common operation when working with APIs, in most cases data is serialised into the JSON format, in C#, many libraries can perform serialisation, including C#'s System.Text.Json library. ","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"8bbd1128-8873-43f9-9eda-4e3c1ec97c80","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Objects are serialized by using reflection to iterate fields and properties, appending \"{CamelCase(fieldName)}\" which would convert a field like FirstName => firstName which matches JavaScript conventions on variables and property names. ","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"2f4f836f-ed46-4bfc-b1e7-a932b6979ed0","component":"Text","data":{"content":"It then recursively serializes the value, if the value is a string, it appends the string, same for number, boolean, null, array or object. ","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"5cf5f53d-a85e-4001-9cc8-e90513356b99","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Now the problem here is that with whatever library you choose to use, you're making tradeoffs in all of them: Newtonsoft.Json gives you rich features and flexibility but at the cost of higher allocations and slower performance; System.Text.Json is faster and more memory-efficient with lower allocations, but it lacks some of the advanced features and flexibility of Newtonsoft; other high-performance serializers may minimize allocations even further but often require more boilerplate, custom converters, or give up compatibility and ease of use.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"899ce111-a3d7-46f0-a4de-3de5cda78d0e","component":"Text","data":{"content":"On top of this, reflection is inherently slow and expensive when called repeatedly. Using it would also add extra code to maintain and would be limited to types that implement a specific interface, since it requires a shared structure to work. So how can we solve this efficiently? And even if implemented using a StringBuilder or similar buffering, it would still generate a significant number of allocations in high-throughput scenarios.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"effa0f60-3c10-4fad-8dec-0fa55b5e08a0","component":"Text","data":{"content":"We run the app with JIT, since AOT would restrict us to using only System.Text.Json because reflection metadata is not preserved in AOT builds. To serialize, we must use reflection to describe the object shapes. There is no way around this, but we can still apply optimizations to reduce its overhead.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]}]
